cards.title: Validation Is the Whole Game
cards.teaser: The LLM's confidence score tells you nothing about whether the refactoring is correct — only mechanical checks do.

runs.text: LLMs do not know when they are wrong. A model that produces a subtly broken refactoring — one that changes a side effect order, drops an edge-case branch, or introduces a name collision in a different scope — will present that output with exactly the same token probabilities as a perfect one. There is no internal signal you can read to tell the difference.

This means the quality of your refactoring pipeline is not determined by the model or the prompt. It is determined by the validation layer that runs after the model outputs something. A well-prompted LLM with no validation is still a liability. A mediocre prompt with thorough validation is safe.
What this implies in practice:

items.text: Treat every LLM output as a candidate, not a result. Nothing ships until at least one gate has cleared it.
The validation budget is not an afterthought — it is the core of the pipeline design. Plan gates before you plan prompts.
LLM confidence, temperature, and model size are secondary variables. The pass/fail rate of your validation suite is the primary variable.
Silent failures are the dangerous ones. A type error or a failing test is recoverable; a behavioral regression that no test catches can sit in production for weeks.

runs.text: The chapters that follow detail every validation gate available. This chapter tells you what each one catches, what it misses, and how to stack them.

"The model said it was equivalent" is not a validation strategy. Asking the LLM to verify its own output catches only the errors it would have caught in the first pass — which is roughly the same set it would have avoided making.
Trusting an LLM to verify its own refactoring is like asking a proofreader to check their own writing — the same blind spots that let the error through the first time will let it through the review.

cards.title: Type Checking as the First Gate
cards.teaser: Type checkers are the cheapest gate you have — they run in seconds, require no test infrastructure, and catch the category of error LLMs introduce most often.

runs.text: When an LLM refactors code, the most common class of failure is a type error: a renamed parameter that doesn't propagate everywhere, a returned type that no longer matches the call site, an imported name that moved. Type checkers catch all of these without executing the code.
The tools, by language:

items.text: mypy
and
pyright

for Python. mypy is more battle-tested on large codebases; pyright (as of 2026-Q2) is faster and has better inference on modern type annotations. Both catch ~60–70% of the LLM errors that surface in typed Python.
tsc --noEmit
for TypeScript. Runs the TypeScript compiler without emitting files — pure type checking. On a 200K-LOC TypeScript monorepo, expect 15–25 seconds.
javac
and
kotlinc
for JVM languages. The compiler is the type checker; a clean compile is the gate.

runs.text: Cost guidance:

items.text: mypy on a 500K-LOC monorepo: ~30 seconds with a warm cache, ~3 minutes cold.
tsc on a 200K-LOC TypeScript project: ~20 seconds.
javac on a Maven project: part of
mvn compile
, typically 10–60 seconds depending on module count.

runs.text: What type checkers miss: runtime values, dynamic dispatch, protocol conformance that isn't captured in annotations, and any behavior that depends on control flow rather than types. A function that accepts
str
and returns
str
but silently truncates its output at 255 characters will pass every type checker and break your downstream system.

explanation.code:
# Run type checking before any test suite
mypy src/ --ignore-missing-imports --no-error-summary
tsc --noEmit --project tsconfig.json

runs.text: Running a type checker on a refactoring is like checking that all the puzzle pieces are the right shape before you try to see if the picture makes sense.

cards.title: Linting and Static Analysis
cards.teaser: Static analysis is the second gate — it catches the code-quality regressions and basic anti-patterns that type checkers ignore and that human reviewers catch inconsistently.

runs.text: LLMs produce syntactically valid, type-correct code that still violates conventions your codebase relies on: unused imports, shadowed names, unreachable branches, dangerous default mutable arguments in Python,
any
casts in TypeScript that defeat type safety, or SQL queries built by string concatenation. Linters and static analyzers catch these cheaply.
The toolchain:

items.text: Ruff
for Python (as of 2026-Q2, the de facto standard). Runs in milliseconds on files a mypy pass takes seconds to process. Covers E, W, F, B, S rule categories — including security lints.
Pylint
for Python when you need deeper semantic checks. Slower than Ruff (~4x), but catches issues Ruff doesn't model (e.g., certain attribute access patterns on dynamic classes).
ESLint
for JavaScript and TypeScript. The configuration surface is large; for refactoring pipelines, run with your project's existing
.eslintrc
so you're catching regressions against your own rules, not against a generic default.
SonarQube

for multi-language, enterprise-scale static analysis. Adds code smell detection, duplication analysis, and security hotspot identification. Expensive to run per-file; best placed at the PR gate rather than per-batch.

runs.text: What static analysis misses: anything that requires executing the code or knowing the runtime state. A lint pass tells you the code is structured correctly; it does not tell you the code behaves correctly.

explanation.code:
# Ruff + ESLint as a fast second gate
ruff check src/ --select E,W,F,B,S
eslint src/ --max-warnings 0

runs.text: Configure your linter to run with
--max-warnings 0
or equivalent. A refactoring pipeline that introduces new warnings is a regression, even if no errors fire.

Static analysis is the copy editor pass — it catches the grammatical mistakes and house-style violations that don't change the meaning of what you're saying but do indicate the author wasn't paying full attention.

cards.title: Unit Test Runs
cards.teaser: Unit tests are what the LLM is supposed to preserve — a red test after refactoring is a direct signal that something changed that shouldn't have.

runs.text: A refactoring, by definition, changes structure without changing observable behavior. Your unit tests encode that observable behavior at the function and class level. A clean unit test run after an LLM refactoring does not prove the refactoring is correct, but a failing unit test proves it is wrong.
The tools:

items.text: pytest
for Python. Run with
-x
to stop at first failure and
--tb=short
for compact tracebacks. In a CI pipeline hitting hundreds of files, full tracebacks waste log space.
vitest
for JavaScript and TypeScript. As of 2026-Q2, vitest's native ESM support and watch mode make it the standard for modern TypeScript projects.
JUnit 5
for Java and Kotlin, typically invoked via Maven (
mvn test
) or Gradle (
./gradlew test

runs.text: What to watch for:

items.text: Flaky tests will mask real failures.

Before using your test suite as a refactoring gate, know which tests are flaky. A pipeline that sees a red test and retries automatically will silently accept a real regression if the flaky test and the broken test happen to coexist.
Test coverage gaps are invisible here.
A suite with 40% coverage passing cleanly says nothing about the uncovered 60%. Coverage signals are separate — see the mutation testing card.
Red tests mean stop.

In a batch pipeline, a unit test failure should halt that file's progression and flag it for human review. Auto-correcting and retrying with the LLM is high-risk; the model may fix the symptom while preserving the underlying behavioral change.

runs.text: Running unit tests after a refactoring is like testing a bridge that's been structurally reinforced — if it still carries the same weight as before, you haven't weakened it; if it doesn't, you've introduced a failure regardless of what the blueprints look like.

cards.title: Integration Test Runs
cards.teaser: Integration tests catch the failures unit tests can't see — the ones that only emerge when components interact — but they're expensive enough that you have to decide carefully when to run them.

runs.text: Unit tests verify components in isolation. Integration tests verify that components work together: the service layer talks to the database correctly, the API serializes responses the client can parse, the event handler triggers the downstream job it's supposed to trigger. LLM refactorings can break these contracts while leaving every unit test green — particularly when the refactoring touches an interface boundary.
The cost profile:

items.text: A typical unit test suite: 5–60 seconds.
A typical integration test suite: 5–30 minutes, often longer when it involves database setup, network calls, or container orchestration.

runs.text: Running full integration tests on every file in a 10,000-file batch refactoring is not realistic. The practical pattern:

items.text: Run type checking and unit tests on every batch.
Run integration tests on every pull request, not on every individual file batch.
Run integration tests immediately on any file that contains an interface, an API handler, a database query, or a message queue consumer — these are the files where integration breakage is likeliest.

runs.text: What integration tests miss: race conditions under real production load, edge cases in external dependency behavior, and anything that only emerges at scale. Integration tests running against a test database do not tell you what happens when the production database is under write pressure.

If your integration test suite takes more than 30 minutes, consider splitting it into a fast tier (contract tests, happy-path integration) that runs on every PR and a slow tier (full end-to-end flows) that runs nightly.

Running integration tests after a refactoring is like testing a plumbing renovation not just by checking each pipe in isolation but by turning the water on and verifying it flows through the whole system without leaking at any joint.

cards.title: Golden Master Testing
cards.teaser: When the correct behavior is too complex to assert directly, capture it as a snapshot — and use that snapshot as the spec the refactoring must match.

runs.text: Some code produces output that is correct but not easily expressible as a hand-written assertion: a complex rendered HTML document, a serialized data structure with hundreds of fields, a formatted report, a generated SQL query. For this code, golden master (or approval) testing works differently: you run the original code, capture the output as a "golden" artifact, then verify the refactored code produces the identical output.
The tools:

items.text: ApprovalTests
(C#, Java, C++, Python) — mature, widely used in legacy system refactoring scenarios.
Vitest snapshots
for TypeScript/JavaScript.
toMatchSnapshot()
generates a
.snap
file on first run and asserts equality on subsequent runs.
Touca
(as of 2026-Q2) — a hosted approval testing platform with diff visualization, test history, and regression alerting. Useful when snapshots are too large to review as text diffs.

runs.text: The critical failure mode of golden masters: they freeze whatever was happening at capture time, including bugs. If you capture a snapshot before fixing a known bug, the snapshot encodes that bug as the expected behavior. Your refactoring will "pass" the golden master test while preserving the buggy behavior. Label your golden master captures with a date and a state description.

The snapshot-as-spec pattern: use golden masters not just for regression testing but as documentation. The captured output is what the system was doing — it answers the question "what does this thing actually produce" more accurately than reading the code.

explanation.code:
# Python with approval tests
python -m pytest tests/golden/ --snapshot-update  # capture
python -m pytest tests/golden/                    # assert

runs.text: A golden master test is like taking a photograph of a room before you renovate it — after the renovation, you can compare room to photograph and confirm nothing you weren't supposed to change has moved.

cards.title: Semantic Diff Tools
cards.teaser: Text diffs tell you what characters changed; semantic diffs tell you what the code's meaning changed — and for LLM refactorings, the distinction matters.

runs.text: A standard
git diff

on a refactored file shows you line-level changes. It cannot distinguish between a rename that is purely cosmetic and a rename that changes which variable a closure closes over. It cannot tell you that two structurally different implementations of a loop are semantically equivalent. Semantic diff tools operate on the AST rather than the text.
The tools:

items.text: gumtree

— a tree-based diff tool that maps AST nodes between two versions and produces edit scripts at the AST level rather than the line level. Supports Python, Java, JavaScript, C, and others. Useful for understanding what structural operations the LLM actually performed.
semantic-diff

— a family of tools (some language-specific, some general) that classify changes by type: renaming, reordering, extraction, inlining. Helps you verify that the change pattern matches the intended refactoring operation.
AST-level diffing built into language toolchains: Python's
ast
module, TypeScript's compiler API, Java's JavaParser — all allow you to write custom equivalence checks that go beyond what text diffing can see.

runs.text: What semantic diffs catch that text diffs miss:

items.text: A variable was renamed locally but the rename didn't propagate to a sibling closure.
An expression was restructured in a way that changes operator precedence.
A loop body was reordered in a way that changes execution order of side effects.

runs.text: What semantic diffs still miss: runtime behavior. Two ASTs can be structurally different and semantically equivalent, or structurally similar and behaviorally different when runtime values are considered.

Use gumtree as a triage tool: if the semantic diff shows only rename and reorder operations, the risk profile is low. If it shows insertions and deletions of logic nodes, escalate to behavior equivalence testing.
A semantic diff is like having a translator compare two speeches — not just checking if the words match, but checking if the meaning does, even when the sentences are phrased differently.

cards.title: Behavior-Equivalence Testing
cards.teaser: Run the original and refactored code side by side on the same inputs and compare outputs — this is the most direct test of equivalence available, and it scales.

runs.text: Behavior-equivalence testing (sometimes called differential testing or A/B execution) runs the pre-refactoring and post-refactoring code on a shared corpus of inputs and compares their outputs. If they agree on every input, the refactoring is behaviorally equivalent. If they disagree, you have an exact example of the divergence.
How to set it up:

items.text: Keep the original code path reachable, even temporarily. This might mean running both versions as separate modules, separate service instances, or separate functions within the same test harness.
Build an input corpus from real call logs, test fixtures, fuzzer output, or sampled production data. The coverage of the corpus determines how much the equivalence test is worth.
Compare outputs structurally, not as raw strings — a JSON response with keys in different order is equivalent; a response with a missing key is not.

runs.text: The shadow traffic pattern at scale: for services, this means routing a copy of production traffic to both the original and refactored implementations and comparing responses asynchronously. The user gets the original response; the diff is logged for analysis. This is expensive operationally but provides the highest-fidelity validation available.

What this approach misses: non-deterministic behavior (functions that produce different outputs on the same input due to timestamps, UUIDs, or random seeds), side effects that aren't captured in the return value (database writes, logging, metric emissions), and performance regressions that don't manifest as output differences.

explanation.code:
# Simple equivalence runner (pseudocode structure)
# for each input in corpus:
#   original_output = run_original(input)
#   refactored_output = run_refactored(input)
#   assert_equivalent(original_output, refactored_output)

runs.text: Behavior-equivalence testing is like sending two translators to interpret the same speech and comparing their transcripts — if both versions say the same thing, the translation preserved the meaning.

cards.title: Property-Based Testing
cards.teaser: Instead of testing specific cases, property-based testing generates hundreds of random inputs and verifies that invariants the refactoring should preserve hold across all of them.

runs.text: Unit tests verify specific known inputs. Property-based tests verify that certain properties hold for all valid inputs in a space — and a randomized search finds inputs you wouldn't have written by hand. For refactoring validation, the question is: what properties should this code preserve regardless of what the LLM changed?
The tools:

items.text: Hypothesis
for Python. As of 2026-Q2, the most widely used property-based testing library. Integrates with pytest. Its shrinking engine reduces failing inputs to minimal counterexamples.
fast-check
for JavaScript and TypeScript. Supports stateful model-based testing in addition to pure property tests.
jqwik
for JVM (Java and Kotlin). Annotation-based property tests that integrate with JUnit 5.

runs.text: Properties worth testing after a refactoring:

items.text: Idempotency: applying the function twice produces the same result as applying it once.
Commutativity: where applicable, order of inputs doesn't change the output.
Round-tripping: serializing then deserializing produces the original value.
Monotonicity: larger inputs produce larger (or smaller) outputs, if that's an invariant of the domain.
No new exceptions: the refactored code should not throw on inputs that the original handled gracefully.

runs.text: What property-based testing misses: properties you didn't think to specify. The framework generates inputs aggressively, but it only checks what you tell it to check.
Run Hypothesis with a fixed database seed in CI so that the same corpus is checked on every run, and failures are reproducible without re-seeding.

Property-based testing is like stress-testing a chair not by sitting in it yourself, but by having a machine apply thousands of different weights from different angles — it finds the failure point you never thought to try.

cards.title: Mutation Testing as a Coverage Signal
cards.teaser: Before trusting your test suite as a refactoring gate, verify that it actually catches changes — mutation testing tells you how many of your tests would pass even if the code were broken.

runs.text: A test suite with 80% line coverage can still be nearly useless as a refactoring gate if the tests don't contain meaningful assertions. Mutation testing validates your tests by deliberately introducing small bugs — mutations — into the code and checking whether any test fails. If tests pass with a mutation present, the mutation survived, meaning your tests are not catching that class of change.
The tools:

items.text: Stryker
for JavaScript and TypeScript (and .NET). As of 2026-Q2, the most actively maintained mutation testing framework for the JS ecosystem.
PIT (Pitest)
for Java and Kotlin. Integrates with Maven and Gradle. Generates a mutation coverage report alongside standard coverage.
Mutmut
for Python. Slower than PIT or Stryker, but straightforward to configure for existing pytest setups.

runs.text: Why this matters for LLM refactoring pipelines: if you're using your test suite as a pass/fail gate, you need to know how much to trust it. A mutation score of 30% means 70% of single-line code changes would go undetected. That's not a gate — that's a filter with most of the mesh missing.
Run mutation testing once before you begin a mass refactoring project, not on every batch. Use the result to:

items.text: Identify modules where the test coverage is too weak to trust as a gate.
Decide which modules need test augmentation before automated refactoring.
Set a floor: "we will not run automated refactoring on any module with a mutation score below 60%."

runs.text: Mutation testing is expensive — Stryker on a large project runs 20–90 minutes. It is not a per-batch gate; it is a pre-campaign audit.

Mutation testing is like checking whether your smoke detectors actually work by deliberately setting off a small, controlled fire — if the alarm doesn't sound, you know the detector wouldn't have saved you in a real emergency.

cards.title: Runtime Canaries
cards.teaser: Deploy refactored code behind a feature flag to a small slice of real traffic, and compare live behavior to the original — this is the validation gate that catches what no offline test can.

runs.text: Every offline validation gate operates on test inputs, test environments, and test data. Real production traffic has characteristics that test suites do not model: edge-case user inputs, database states accumulated over years, third-party API responses that vary, and concurrent access patterns that no integration test replicates. A runtime canary exposes the refactored code to a controlled slice of that real traffic and monitors for divergence.
The mechanics:

items.text: Deploy the refactored code behind a feature flag.
Route 1–5% of traffic to the refactored path.
Monitor for error rate increases, latency regressions, and unexpected exception types.
Compare key business metrics (not just technical metrics) between the canary and the control.
Gradually increase traffic allocation if no anomalies appear; roll back immediately if they do.

runs.text: What runtime canaries catch that offline tests miss:

items.text: Behavioral differences under production data that has edge cases no test fixture covers.
Performance regressions (the refactored code is correct but 3x slower on a particular query pattern).
Interaction effects with infrastructure (different memory allocation behavior, different GC pressure).

runs.text: What runtime canaries miss: correctness issues that affect rare user paths not present in the initial canary traffic slice, and issues that only manifest at high traffic volumes.
This card frames the pattern; Chapter 8 covers the full risk management layer around canary deployments, including rollback triggers and traffic shaping strategies.

A canary with 1% traffic exposure that runs for 30 minutes is not a meaningful validation for paths that only execute once per day. Size canary duration and traffic fraction to the execution frequency of the code you changed.

A runtime canary is like opening a new branch of a restaurant with a small number of invited guests before the public opening — you're serving real food to real people, and their reactions tell you things a test kitchen never could.

cards.title: Validation Cost vs. Catch Rate
cards.teaser: Every gate takes time; running all gates on every file is impossible — the design question is which gates go where, and what you're willing to miss.

runs.text: A complete validation stack for one file might look like: type check (30 seconds), lint (5 seconds), unit tests (60 seconds), property tests (90 seconds), integration tests (20 minutes), semantic diff (10 seconds), golden master comparison (30 seconds). That's over 22 minutes per file. At 10,000 files, that's 153 days of sequential validation. The pipeline must be tiered.
A practical tiering model:

items.text: Per-file, synchronous (every file, immediately after LLM output):
type check, lint. Total: under 60 seconds. Catches ~50–60% of LLM errors. Files that fail this gate are discarded or flagged without proceeding further.
Per-batch (every 50–200 files, as a batch completes):
unit tests. Catches the next largest category of regressions. A batch-level failure halts that batch and sends it for human triage.
Per-pull-request:
integration tests, semantic diff, golden master checks. Runs once before code merges. The full bill of checking happens here, just not per-file.
Post-deploy, ongoing:
runtime canary. The final gate; catches what everything else misses.

runs.text: The cost-of-missing calculation: the cost of a missed bug in a type signature is low (usually caught in code review or the next test run). The cost of a missed bug in a payment processing function that passes all offline tests but fails on a rare production data pattern is potentially very high. Let the risk profile of the code drive gate placement, not the convenience of the pipeline author.

Reproducibility note: every gate in this stack must produce the same result tomorrow for the same input. Fix your test random order seeds. Pin dependency versions. Capture environment state. A validation failure that you cannot reproduce is not actionable.

explanation.code:
# Tiered gate invocation sketch
tsc --noEmit && ruff check src/          # gate 1: per-file
pytest -x --tb=short tests/unit/         # gate 2: per-batch
pytest tests/integration/ --timeout=1800 # gate 3: per-PR

runs.text: Choosing which validation gates to run where is like choosing how many security checkpoints to place in an airport — you can inspect every bag at every door, but the terminal will stop moving; the art is placing the thorough checks where the risk is highest and the fast checks everywhere else.
